questions.question: Where does Optional<T> earn its keep in Java?
choices: As a field type, replacing nullable fields.
As a parameter type, to force callers to wrap.
As a return type, signalling 'this method may have no answer'.
As a collection type, replacing empty collections.
questions.explanation: Optional was designed for return types - communicating absence to the caller before they dereference. Fields, parameters, and Optional<Collection> are anti-patterns: empty collections are already a no-result signal, and Optional fields add allocations and serialization friction.

questions.question: Which of these expressions correctly produces a String 'anonymous' when the user is not found?
questions.explanation: Compose Optional with .map (transform if present) and .orElse (default if absent). The other forms either use non-existent Java syntax (?? ?:), or duplicate the lookup, or defeat the type with .get().

questions.question: What is the practical difference between Java's Optional<T> approach and C#'s nullable reference types (NRT)?
choices: They are equivalent; both are compile-time annotations.
Optional is a runtime wrapper at API boundaries; NRT is a compile-time static analysis on plain reference types. Different mechanisms, similar intent.
Optional adds runtime null checks; NRT changes the JVM's handling of null.
NRT is a runtime feature; Optional is a compile-time annotation.
questions.explanation: Optional is a concrete wrapper type - every Optional<T> is an allocation, and presence is checked at runtime. C# NRT is purely a compiler analysis with no runtime effect. They both target the 'forgot to handle absence' bug class, but via different mechanisms.
